title: Concrete Paver Outdoor Coffee Table
summary: An outdoor coffee table topped with four 12x12 concrete pavers glued into a 2x2 frame — a weatherproof stone-look top for garden-center money.
description:
Outdoor coffee tables have a materials problem: a wood top weathers, glass breaks, and real stone costs real money. This design's answer is sitting in a pallet at every garden center — 12x12 concrete pavers, four of them, glued into a slatted 2x2 frame. The result reads as a stone-top table, shrugs off rain and sun and dropped drinks, and the 'stone' costs less than the screws.

The base is all 2x2 and pocket holes: two leg sets, long rails connecting them, a lower slat shelf, and top supports. One layout detail carries the design — the TOP support slats have their pocket holes drilled facing UP, and the bottom shelf slats have theirs facing DOWN, so every screw hole ends up hidden (the top's by the pavers sitting on them, the shelf's by facing the ground). Same joint, two orientations, zero visible holes.

Mind the weight, in both directions. Four 12x12 pavers add up to roughly 40–50 pounds, which is exactly why this table won't blow over in a storm and also why the frame gets glue at every joint and the pavers get construction adhesive — a paver sliding off a racked frame is a broken toe with a landscaping backstory. Build the frame square, glue the pavers with a heavy-duty adhesive (the source names GE Iron Grip; any polyurethane construction adhesive rated for concrete works), and let it cure a full day before the first drink lands on it.

Stain the wood with a penetrating exterior stain before the pavers go in — much easier without 50 pounds of concrete in the way.

tools.note: 1-1/2" setting, 2-1/2" screws, every joint. Exterior-rated screws for outdoor life.
Eighteen 2x2 crosscuts. A miter saw is faster.

materials.name: Concrete pavers, 12" x 12"
materials.note: Garden-center stock. Pick four flat ones from the same pallet so thickness matches.

materials.name: 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: The entire frame. Straight ones only — a bowed 2x2 rocks a stone top.

materials.name: Heavy-duty construction adhesive
materials.note: Polyurethane, rated for concrete — GE Iron Grip or equivalent. This is what keeps 50 lb of paver married to the frame.

materials.name: Penetrating exterior stain
materials.note: Applied BEFORE the pavers go in.

cutList.note: Rails, shelf slats, and top supports — the repetition is the build.

steps.title: Build two leg sets
steps.body:
Join two 21" rails between two legs with 1-1/2" pocket holes, 2-1/2" exterior pocket screws, and glue — one rail at the top, one near the bottom. Build the second set identical (stack-check them).

Glue at every joint isn't optional on this one: the frame will spend its life holding concrete overhead.

steps.title: Connect the leg sets
steps.body: Join the two leg sets with long rails front and back, top and bottom — pocket holes again. Check the frame for square by diagonals; the pavers are rigid squares and will announce any parallelogram in the frame with gaps at the corners.

steps.title: Bottom shelf slats — holes down
steps.body: Install the lower shelf slats with their pocket holes facing DOWN, hidden toward the ground. Even spacing; this shelf holds citronella candles and paperbacks, not pavers, so the gaps are fine.

steps.title: Top supports — holes UP — then stain, then stone
steps.body:
Install the top support slats with pocket holes facing UP: the pavers will sit on and hide them, which is the trick that leaves this table with zero visible fasteners. Space the supports so every paver edge lands on wood.

Stain everything now — penetrating exterior stain, all faces — and let it dry. Then bed the four pavers in generous beads of construction adhesive, seat them tight together, and leave the table alone for 24 hours. The adhesive is structural: a loose paver on a knee-height table is a genuine hazard, so don't economize on the beads.

images.alt: Outdoor coffee table with four concrete pavers set as the tabletop
